description: The quantifier expressing an indefinite nonempty subset or amount: some people, some days, or some water. 'Soli' has no fixed numerical lower or upper boundary and does not by itself mean few, enough, or a particular proportion. It is appropriate when identifying an unspecified part is more relevant than giving its exact size.
sound_symbolism: The word begins with 's', a soft sibilant suggesting elements scattered from a larger set. The round 'o' gathers an unspecified portion, and the liquid 'l' flows into bright 'i' without closing that portion into a fixed count. The image supports an indefinite subset; it does not encode fewness or sufficiency.
grammatical_notes: Functions as a quantifier preceding nouns: 'soli miona' (some people), 'soli philo' (some days), and 'soli phialu' (some water). Never combines with plural 'lo' because the quantifier already marks quantity, and does not take a classifier; classifiers belong to numeral phrases and 'wia' questions. Use 'phina' to assert few, 'sheloi' to assert many, and a bare scale to estimate around a numerical center.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Leaving a subset indefinite can support attention to what is relevant without attachment to an unnecessary count. 'Soli' does not imply that precision causes suffering or that the amount is sufficient.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: 'Soli' can avoid unsupported precision, but an indefinite subset can also conceal exclusion or unequal distribution. Context determines whether a count or named group is needed.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Everyday practical speech uses indefinite amounts alongside exact counts and measures. 'Soli' supplies one such form without assigning a single counting philosophy to traditional life.
